Multiple rounds of thunderstorms produced very heavy rainfall across the Ozarks over the course of a week.  A persistent trough over the central plains brought multiple upper level storm systems over the region which produced intense thunderstorms with very heavy rainfall.  Some areas saw storm total rainfall amounts up to a foot or more.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Route AA was closed due to flooding.||The total cost estimate for flooding damages for Stone County for this entire episode has been included.  This includes roads, bridges, and structures which were affected.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms organized into lines and bows that produced widespread damaging winds. A shortwave trough over the central and southern high Plains moved northeast through the Mid-Mississippi Valley into the upper Great Lakes. A surface low attendant to this system moved from west central Missouri northeast to north of St. Louis. The trailing cold front advanced southeast across the Lower Ohio Valley and served as the primary focus for thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: In Silva, a pontoon boat, two vehicles, and a carport were damaged. The 26-foot pontoon boat was lifted off a trailer and flipped over. The carport was blown about one-eighth of a mile. The two vehicles received minor damage from the airborne objects. Shingles were blown off a garage and shed. Large trees were blown down. U.S. Highway 67 was blocked by downed power lines.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low over western Kansas combined with a cold front moved into the region and produced severe thunderstorms.  Numerous severe storms and a few supercells produced very large hail and high wind gusts as the front moved through the region.

An upper level low over western Kansas combined with a cold front moved into the region and produced severe thunderstorms.  Numerous severe storms and a few supercells produced very large hail and high wind gusts as the front moved through the region.

A warm front was lifting northward through the region, triggering numerous showers and thunderstorms.  Some of the storms produced tornadoes as well as a lot of large hail.  There were 6 tornadoes with this event, including an EF3.

Heavy rainfall in March set the stage for major flooding when record-setting rains fell in April and May. At Cape Girardeau, 31.85 inches of rain fell between March and May. This set the record for the wettest meteorological spring (March through May) and was 68 percent of the normal rainfall for the entire year. The result of all this rain was rapid and dramatic rises on rivers. Record flood crests occurred on some rivers, inundating numerous homes and businesses. There were major impacts to transportation both on land and water. There were numerous closed roads across the region, leading to often lengthy detours. Because of the high water, there were numerous day and nighttime restrictions to barge traffic. Hundreds of thousands of acres of farmland were flooded. Crops were late getting planted.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Moderate flooding occurred on the Black River. At the Poplar Bluff river gage, the river crested at 20.21 feet on May 3. Water overflowing levees that were damaged in April kept the crest from being higher. On Highway 53 southeast of Poplar Bluff, the National Guard rescued an elderly woman from chest-deep water after she drove into a flooded stretch of road. The highway was closed at the time. Some low-lying woods and fields near the river were flooded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low over western Kansas combined with a cold front moved into the region and produced severe thunderstorms.  Numerous severe storms and a few supercells produced very large hail and high wind gusts as the front moved through the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency manager estimated wind gusts up to 60 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving upper level trough and low pressure system across the central plains region moving across the Ozarks caused numerous heavy showers and storms that produced heavy rainfall.  This lead to additional flooding and flash flooding across the Ozarks.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous roads were reported impassable.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An isolated severe thunderstorm occurred ahead of a closed 500 mb low moving east across Missouri. A weak surface low was located over central Illinois, with a cold front trailing southward across the Mississippi Valley.

A weak cool front that became stationary across the Ozarks and southeastern Kansas interacted with a very unstable airmass. Isolated strong to severe pulse storms occurred across the Ozarks. Reports of hail up to golf ball size, isolated wind damage and heavy rainfall were reported.

A frontal boundary across the area and an upper level disturbance moving across the central Plains into the Ozarks caused a cluster of strong to severe storms to develop. Storms produced high winds and large hail across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minor roof damage occurred to the Kum and Go gas station in town.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of thunderstorms moved out of Kansas and across the Missouri Ozarks during the overnight hours. The storms produced isolated reports of hail and wind damage as they moved east.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds were estimated at 60 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak cold front moved into the area triggering showers and thunderstorms.  Some of the storms produced large hail and damaging winds.
A strong cold front moved through the region, triggering showers and thunderstorms. Some of the storms produced severe weather including large hail, damaging winds and an isolated tornado.
Elevated thunderstorms developed north of a warm front.  The storms produced lots of large hail.  Some of the hail stones were baseball size.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail up to baseball size fell in the Mapaville area.  Several cars had their windshields destroyed by the hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front was lifting northward through forecast area.  Severe storms developed along and north of the boundary.  There were numerous reports of large hail with these storms.

A weak upper level disturbance, interacted with a stationary front along the Highway 36 corridor, to produce severe thunderstorms, during the afternoon and evening hours of July 12, 2011. There were several reports of hail and damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At the intersection of 69th Street and Brookside, some street flooding was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of thunderstorms moved east along the Interstate 70 corridor, during the late morning and early afternoon hours of May 24, 2011. There were several reports of hail and high winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm wind gusts were estimated up to 60 mph. One to two inch tree limbs were reported down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very warm and moist airmass had advected northwest into the area, during the morning hours of May 25, 2011, with the development of an intensifying warm front, near the Highway 50 corridor.  Due to ample lift, provided by the strong upper level storm system just west of Kansas City, a little bit of clearing, made the rapid development of thunderstorms along and ahead of the cold front possible, as early as 10 am.  As these storms lifted northward and rotated around the surface area of low pressure, they would rapidly begin to rotate, as they reached the enhanced winds near the warm front.  Once thunderstorm activity crossed over the warm front into northern Missouri, and into a slightly cooler and more stable airmass, they would still indicate rotation, but the environment prevented further tornadic activity.||There were nearly two dozen reports of either funnel clouds or tornadoes, with seven of the tornado reports in Missouri, being confirmed by NWS damage survey crews and/or law enforcement. The tornadoes that occurred, were associated with the smaller cousin of the classic central plains supercell thunderstorm, called a mini- or low-topped supercell thunderstorm. Mini-supercells, typically occur in association with a cold-core upper level low pressure system, and are much smaller in height and areal expanse, than the classic supercell storm. But regardless of their size, mini-supercells still possess the mechanics to produce brief, and seen in the next paragraph about Sedalia, violent tornadoes.  ||An EF-2 tornado, associated with winds up to 135 mph, damaged or destroyed numerous single family homes, and businesses, severely damaged two mobile home parks, blew over several tanker trucks, and injured 20 people, as it roared through the southern part of Sedalia, Missouri. In addition, several school buses, located in the school district bus barn, were damaged by the tornado.

Strong area of low pressure moved into the forecast area with a dry line out ahead of the main cold front.  Storms fired up along and just ahead of the dry line beginning in the early afternoon hours.  There were numerous reports of large hail with isolated damaging winds and tornadoes with this event.

Strong area of low pressure moved into the forecast area with a dry line out ahead of the main cold front.  Storms fired up along and just ahead of the dry line beginning in the early afternoon hours.  There were numerous reports of large hail with isolated damaging winds and tornadoes with this event.

A weak cool front that became stationary across the Ozarks and southeastern Kansas interacted with a very unstable airmass. Isolated strong to severe pulse storms occurred across the Ozarks. Reports of hail up to golf ball size, isolated wind damage and heavy rainfall were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large metal sign was blown over on Highway 71 at County Road DD.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front moved through region. With plenty of moisture, instability and decent low level shear, severe storms developed along the Interstate 70 corridor in Missouri and Illinois.  Numerous reports of large hail and 5 tornadoes occurred with this event.  One of the tornadoes was a long track one that moved through the St. Louis metropolitan area with it being rated an EF4, with only a few minor injuries and no deaths. Also, quite a bit of rain fell during this event and caused flash flooding.  The flash flooding persisted for several days as additional rounds of heavy rain fell over the same region through the 26th of April.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Between 6 and 9 inches fell over several days on already saturated grounds causing flash flooding.  Numerous roads were flooded including U.S. Highway 67 in Farmington.  Also, there was three feet of flowing water near the intersection of Veterans Drive and East College Street.  Intersection of Highways OO and DD, just southwest of Libertyville was closed due to flooding.  The Big River flooded well out of its banks making Turkey Creek Road impassable north of Bonne Terre.  Several water rescues had to be performed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cluster of severe thunderstorms moved across north central Missouri, during the evening hours of June 14, 2011, into the early morning hours of June 15, 2011. There was one report of a tornado, and several reports of hail and damaging winds.
A strong cold front was moving through the region.  It triggered showers and thunderstorms...with numerous reports of large hail.

A strong storm system was approaching the region. Numerous storms developed ahead of the system during the late morning and afternoon hours on May 23rd.  There were numerous reports of damaging winds and large hail.

A weak cool front became stationary across central Missouri interacting with a very unstable airmass. Isolated strong to severe pulse storms occurred across the Ozarks which produced isolated wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A television station weather spotter reported a tree blown down and small hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Upper level energy embedded in northwestern flow over the Ozarks interacted with a very unstable airmass over the region. Strong to severe storms produced hail up to quarter size and scattered wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A man was killed from a tree that fell on his car while driving. This occurred on County Road 440 one tenth of a mile west of Highway 19 about 3 miles north of Winona.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak frontal boundary combined with summer daytime heating, helped develop isolated strong to severe storms which caused localized downbursts and large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public in Sarcoxie reported at least 60 to 70 mph wind gusts.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front, combined with an upper level disturbance, caused a squall line to move east across the area, during the evening hours of June 26, 2011. Damaging winds and large hail were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm wind gusts were estimated up to 70 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front approaching the region, caused a severe thunderstorm outbreak, during the afternoon and evening hours of June 9, 2011. There were numerous reports of hail and damaging winds.
Scattered severe storms developed ahead of an approaching weather system.  Several reports of large hail and damaging winds occurred with these storms.

A powerful mid-level shortwave trough ejected east-northeast across the southern Plains, reaching the lower and mid Mississippi Valley during the night. In the lower levels, a low intensified as it tracked from Oklahoma into southern Missouri during the day, then across the Lower Ohio Valley in the evening. To the east of the low, a warm front advanced northward to the Lower Ohio Valley. Storms moving east-northeast evolved into an extensive QLCS (quasi-linear convective system) that produced scattered wind damage in New Madrid County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The path width of this downburst was about 75 yards, and the path length was about 300 yards. Two well-constructed barns were destroyed. There was shingle and siding damage to at least five homes. Tree limbs were down, and a privacy fence was down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful mid-level shortwave trough ejected east-northeast across the southern Plains, reaching the lower and mid Mississippi Valley during the night. In the lower levels, a low intensified as it tracked from Oklahoma into southern Missouri during the day, then across the Lower Ohio Valley in the evening. To the east of the low, a warm front advanced northward to the Lower Ohio Valley. Storms moving east-northeast evolved into an extensive QLCS (quasi-linear convective system) that produced scattered wind damage in New Madrid County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A funnel cloud was observed near the downburst damage site by numerous people.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms which developed during the evening hours moved across the Ozarks and produced hail and damaging winds. The storms were initially multi-cellular in mode with a few embedded supercells, before transitioning into a mix of multicell clusters and line segments. The strongest line segment developed over north central Christian County and south central Greene County, which quickly moved to the northeast and bowed out.  A leading notch was warned on for the potential for a tornado and a damage path coincided with this feature. Another second, very strong line segment moved through the Rolla area around 0430z, resulting in reports of power poles down and roof damage to an apartment complex.

A weak upper level disturbance, interacted with a stationary front along the Highway 36 corridor, to produce severe thunderstorms, during the afternoon and evening hours of July 12, 2011. There were several reports of hail and damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several large tree branches were reported down. Thunderstorm wind gusts were estimated up to 60 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm complex developed along a warm front over the forecast area.  There were several reports of damaging winds and flash flooding with these storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds blew down several power lines.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front moved into central Missouri during the afternoon and evening hours of February 27, 2011. This warm front generated severe thunderstorms, mainly south of a Moberly to Kansas City line. Large hail and damaging winds were reported.

A surface low pressure center developed northeastward from south central Oklahoma to southwest Missouri. A gradual increase in the warm sector southerly low level jet occurred as the surface low deepened. Increasing vertical wind shear in the moist and unstable warm sector supported the development of short lines of thunderstorms with embedded lewps and supercell structures.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts were estimated around 60 mph by law enforcement officers.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front and upper level storm system, moved across Missouri in the|late afternoon and evening hours of March 22, 2011. Severe thunderstorms with|numerous hail reports, were found mainly east of the Highway 65 corridor.
Showers and thunderstorms developed over Missouri ahead of a strong cold front.  Some of the storms became severe with large hail and isolated reports of damaging winds.

Severe thunderstorms developed along and behind a strong cold front during the afternoon and evening. The front interacted with a very moist and unstable atmosphere which combined to produce hail up to baseball size, wind damage and very heavy rainfall. Numerous reports of flooding were reported across the Ozarks.

A quasi-stationary frontal boundary was the main focusing mechanism for a round of significant severe weather over the forecast area.  A strong shortwave moved along the boundary triggering widespread showers and thunderstorms.  Numerous reports of large hail, damaging winds and 22 tornadoes occurred with this system.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tornado touched down along Rosemar Lane causing extensive tree damage. The tornado tracked east across Highway 109 causing minor tree and shingle damage to a few homes before lifting. The width of the damage area averaged 70 yards and was rated EF1. The total path length of the tornado was sixth tenths of a mile.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Numerous clusters of thunderstorms preceded a cold front as it tracked east-southeast across Illinois and southeast Missouri. The storms were aided by a weak but amplifying 500 mb shortwave trough that moved east-southeast toward the middle and upper Mississippi Valley. Several of the storms produced large hail and wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wind gust to 59 mph was measured at the Cape Girardeau airport automated observation site.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The next in a series of thunderstorm complexes developed over southeastern Kansas and slid east across forecast area.  There were numerous reports of large hail and damaging winds, as well as two tornadoes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds uprooted a large tree.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Upper level energy embedded in northwestern flow over the Ozarks interacted with a very unstable airmass over the region. Strong to severe storms produced hail up to quarter size and scattered wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees were reported down on power lines and a car.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A forward-propagating mesoscale convective system brought damaging winds to portions of southeast Missouri. The electric co-operative serving the five affected counties reported 5,450 customers lost power. The thunderstorm complex moved southeast at 40 knots across the Ozark foothills toward the Bootheel. The complex stayed generally southwest of a Greenville to Dexter to New Madrid line. The storms occurred ahead of a weak cold front that extended from central Illinois to central and southwest Missouri. The environment was very moist and moderately to strongly unstable. The storms occurred on the southern fringe of a band of moderately strong west-northwest mid-level flow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: In Van Buren, a large tree was blown down at the county courthouse. In Eastwood, trees were blown down in town, shingles were blown off roofs, and a roof was ripped off a front porch and blown over the house into the back yard. Tree damage was reported at various other locations near these communities.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms were observed across the area, during the afternoon hours of August 7, 2011. A cold front moved southeast across the region, with hail and damaging winds reported.
An upper level disturbance interacted with a warm front across the Ozarks and produced several strong to severe storms.
A line of thunderstorms moved east along the Interstate 70 corridor, during the late morning and early afternoon hours of May 24, 2011. There were several reports of hail and high winds.

A potent weather system moved across the plains during the afternoon of the 23rd through the 24th. Abundant moisture, instability and lift along a front acted to focus thunderstorm development over the Ozarks region. This produced thunderstorms that developed and trained over many of the same area and produced large hail and heavy rainfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There were several reports of cars dented by hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A quasi-stationary frontal boundary was the main focusing mechanism for a round of significant severe weather over the forecast area.  A strong shortwave moved along the boundary triggering widespread showers and thunderstorms.  Numerous reports of large hail, damaging winds and 22 tornadoes occurred with this system.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tornado touched down in Babler Memorial State Park and caused tree damage southwest of the intersection of Theodore Martin Drive and Guy Park Road in the park, where several large trees where either snapped or uprooted. The tornado moved northeast along Guy Park Road causing more tree damage before lifting. This tornado was rated EF1, had a path length of 0.36 mile and a maximum width of 75 yards.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A quasi-stationary frontal boundary was the main focusing mechanism for a round of significant severe weather over the forecast area.  A strong shortwave moved along the boundary triggering widespread showers and thunderstorms.  Numerous reports of large hail, damaging winds and 22 tornadoes occurred with this system.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A four to five inch diameter tree was snapped off at the base at the National Weather Service office.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stalled frontal boundary over the region helped to trigger showers and thunderstorms.  Some of the storms produced severe weather and flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds blew down several power lines around town.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm system caused strong thunderstorms, in the afternoon hours of March 4, 2011. Penny and nickel sized hail was reported from Cass and Jackson Counties.

A cold front helped to generate severe thunderstorms, across portions of Missouri, mainly along the Interstate 70 corridor, in the afternoon hours of May 22, 2011. There were several reports of tornadoes, along with reports of hail and damaging wind.

A frontal boundary across the area and an upper level disturbance moving across the central Plains into the Ozarks caused a cluster of strong to severe storms to develop. Storms produced high winds and large hail across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large tree was reported down across Highway 19 north.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Numerous strong to severe thunderstorms occurred in the warm sector of a low pressure system over central Missouri. The air mass in the warm sector was very unstable, with afternoon mixed-layer cape values in the 2500 to 4000 j/kg range. A 65 to 75 knot mid level jet was located across Arkansas, with the nose of the jet moving into western Kentucky. This feature enhanced the lift and strong deep-layer shear already in place, creating an environment very favorable for supercells and tornadoes. A 40 to 55 knot low level jet across eastern Arkansas moved into western Kentucky during the evening, resulting in a long-track strong tornado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported a brief touchdown occurred at Highway N and Highway KK, south of Oxly.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong area of low pressure moved into the forecast area with a dry line out ahead of the main cold front.  Storms fired up along and just ahead of the dry line beginning in the early afternoon hours.  There were numerous reports of large hail with isolated damaging winds and tornadoes with this event.

Severe thunderstorms developed along a surface boundary, that was draped across west central Missouri, during the late evening hours of June 18th, into the early morning hours of June 19th, 2011. Hail, damaging winds, and flash flooding were observed.
Elevated convection fired up over the region on nose of strong low level jet.  Numerous reports of large hail and isolated flash flooding were reported with these storms.
Elevated thunderstorms developed north of a warm front.  The storms produced lots of large hail.  Some of the hail stones were baseball size.
Elevated convection fired up over the region on nose of strong low level jet.  Numerous reports of large hail and isolated flash flooding were reported with these storms.
A warm front, combined with an upper level disturbance, caused a squall line to move east across the area, during the evening hours of June 26, 2011. Damaging winds and large hail were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At 1850CST the media reported a brief EF0 tornado touchdown, 5 miles west of Maryville. The tornado moved just a little to the east over open fields, and then lifted at 1851CST, nearly 5 miles east of Maryville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front was lifting northward through the region, triggering numerous showers and thunderstorms.  Some of the storms produced tornadoes as well as a lot of large hail.  There were 6 tornadoes with this event, including an EF3.

A warm front was lifting northward through the region, triggering numerous showers and thunderstorms.  Some of the storms produced tornadoes as well as a lot of large hail.  There were 6 tornadoes with this event, including an EF3.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous reports of hail ranging from one inch to two and a half inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak cool front that became stationary across the Ozarks and southeastern Kansas interacted with a very unstable airmass. Isolated strong to severe pulse storms occurred across the Ozarks which produced isolated wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A window was blown out of a business. A traffic sign was partially detached from its mounting. Numerous power outages knocked out traffic signals.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front caused a few severe thunderstorms over western Missouri, during the evening hours of June 10, 2011. There were several reports of hail.
Elevated thunderstorms developed north of a warm front.  The storms produced lots of large hail.  Some of the hail stones were baseball size.
A warm front lifting northward across the Ozarks produced strong to severe storms which had large hail.

A cold front and upper level storm system, moved across Missouri in the|late afternoon and evening hours of March 22, 2011. Severe thunderstorms with|numerous hail reports, were found mainly east of the Highway 65 corridor.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An outbuilding was reported destroyed, with thunderstorm wind gusts estimated |to 60 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak frontal boundary combined with summer daytime heating, helped develop isolated strong to severe storms which caused localized downbursts and large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A garage door was reported damaged.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stubborn upper level trough over the central and southern Plains region along with a stalled out stationary front over the lower Mississippi Valley produced several rounds of moderate to heavy rainfall especially over the southern and eastern Ozarks.  Rainfall amounts over a two day period averaged from one to four inches with isolated higher amounts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law enforcement reported several low water crossing flooded throughout Douglas County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front across the region was the focus of thunderstorm development.  A mesoscale convective system developed during the evening of June 25th and moved east across the region.  There were numerous reports of large hail, damaging winds and flash flooding.

An upper level low over western Kansas combined with a cold front moved into the region and produced severe thunderstorms.  Numerous severe storms and a few supercells produced very large hail and high wind gusts as the front moved through the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several large limbs were blown down around town along with isolated power outages.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong area of low pressure moved into the forecast area with a dry line out ahead of the main cold front.  Storms fired up along and just ahead of the dry line beginning in the early afternoon hours.  There were numerous reports of large hail with isolated damaging winds and tornadoes with this event.

A quasi-stationary frontal boundary was the main focusing mechanism for a round of significant severe weather over the forecast area.  A strong shortwave moved along the boundary triggering widespread showers and thunderstorms.  Numerous reports of large hail, damaging winds and 22 tornadoes occurred with this system.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds blew down numerous large trees, tree limbs and power lines throughout town.  A couple of businesses sustained minor to moderate roof damage when sections of their roofs were blown off.  Some of the fallen trees landed on homes and vehicles causing minor to moderate damage.  A section of bleachers at the Washington Fairgrounds were folded over.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A vigorous shortwave moving over the Ozarks interacted with a moderately unstable airmass over the region. Strong to severe storms occurred across the Ozarks with reports of hail up to half dollar size.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Penny size hail covered the ground.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front was moving through the area triggering showers and thunderstorms.  There were numerous reports of large hail, as well as damaging winds and flash flooding.
Thunderstorms developed over central Missouri north of the 850 mb warm front.  A number of large hail reports were received.

A strong storm system was approaching the region. Numerous storms developed ahead of the system during the late morning and afternoon hours on May 23rd.  There were numerous reports of damaging winds and large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds uprooted several trees.  One tree was two feet in diameter.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mesoscale convective complex developed over Southwest Missouri during the early morning hours of June 13th, 2011. The remnants of the complex moved into the Mid-South during the late morning into the afternoon hours. Additional thunderstorms developed along old outflow boundaries of the complex. Some of these thunderstorms became severe and produced large hail and damaging winds.

A surface low pressure center developed northeastward from south central Oklahoma to southwest Missouri. A gradual increase in the warm sector southerly low level jet occurred as the surface low deepened. Increasing vertical wind shear in the moist and unstable warm sector supported the development of short lines of thunderstorms with embedded lewps and supercell structures.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were blown down and snapped off.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated storms developed over northeast Missouri and west central Illinois. There were some reports of wind damage with these storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds blew down several large tree limbs around town.  Also, a ham radio tower was bent over by the winds.
